04 The 3 Year Target - YouTube.mp4
The first step in filling out your company's clarity compass is to define your three-year target. A three-year target is a crystal clear business objective that outlines what the company's top line revenue, bottom line profit, and total enterprise value will be in three years from the date the goal is set. Now, some companies might refer to this as their mission, but again, since no one is in complete agreement on what that term even means, we just call it your three-year target. Now, the tool that we use— to define and track our three-year target— is the 12Q planning grid. It's called the 12Q planning grid because it tracks our company's growth over 12 quarters, which, if you do the math, is also known as three years. Here's how it works.
First, we need to identify your starting point. In other words, what is your core annual revenue and profit margin today? If you aren't sure, that's okay. Just guess. For now, because you can always go back and double check it later. Now, once you have your starting point, just enter it into the top left section of the 12Q planning grid. Next, we need to project out where you want your company to be in three years from today in terms of both annual revenue and profitability. Let's start with top line revenue. Now, maybe you already know exactly where you want to be in three years. But if not, here are some benchmarks that should help. For VC-backed companies hoping to achieve unicorn status and and hypergrowth, their goal is triple-triple, double-double.
In other words, they need to triple their revenue the first two years after launch, and then double— each of the next two years. If they can pull this off, they'll achieve a hundred million dollars in revenue in seven years or less. And that coveted billion-dollar unicorn stat. valuation. Now, I'm not saying this can't happen for you, but as a general rule, going triple, triple, double, double requires a lot of outside capital. A massively growing market and near perfect execution. It does happen, but it's not in the cards for most bootstrap businesses. So I would be slow to... recommend this model for your three-year growth target. Next up, we have what's known as rapid growth. For rapid growth companies, the goal is double-double 75%.
In other words, their sales need to double year over year for two years in a row, and then nearly double again in year three. This level of growth is possible for bootstrap companies, but it typically only occurs in the earliest stages of the business, when revenue numbers are still relatively small. A more conservative goal would be to take the steady growth option. As a steady growth company, your goal is to grow 25 to 50% year over year, essentially, into perpetuity. If you've heard the story of the tortoise and the hare, this is definitely the tortoise option. Don't forget, slow and steady does win the race. So there's... shame in taking the steady growth route. In fact, as you're about to see, this is the option that I recommend most often.
Last on the list is mature growth. Mature growth phase, your goal is to grow 10 to 20 percent per year. Now, as the name suggests, this is a fine goal for a legacy businesses in slow-growing markets. But this level of growth does leave you vulnerable to disruption, and it can make it challenging to attract and retain the best talent. Because, let's face it, great people want to work at great companies, and great companies are growing companies. So these are your benchmarks. Hyper growth, rapid growth, steady growth, and mature growth. So those are really helpful, but at The Scalable Company, we take a slightly different path and encourage our clients to choose between two growth plans. They are: top to bottom, and... Double in three. Top to bottom is my favorite.
This is where you commit to turning your current top line revenue into your three-year bottom line profit while still maintaining the same or better profit margin. Now, imagine for a moment: How great it would be if you could pocket 100% of what you generated in sales this year. What could you do with that kind of cash? Would you put directly into your pocket? Top to bottom is the gold standard, but if you're afraid it might be a little too aggressive, then consider doubling three. Jess with double in three, you're committing to at least double your top line revenue and your bottom line profit in three years or less. If doubling your sales and profit— in three years— still seems aggressive, then consider this. A 26% year-over-year increase is enough to double in three years.
Yep, just grow. 26% three years in a row and your business will be twice as large as when you started. If you don't believe me, just do the math on that. Now, if you can bump that—year-over-year growth— to 40%, you'll double in only two years. And how about this stat? If you can grow your sales just 3% a month, every month, you'll double your revenue in just two years. Yep, if you can manage just a paltry 3% month-over-month growth, and sustain it over a two-year period, your business will double. And again, it's not a question of if, it's just math. Now, all the scenarios I just mentioned fall in that steady growth category, that kind of tortoise category. My hope is...
that if nothing else, this little exercise proves that you can at the very least double your business over the next three years, even if you're just maintaining steady growth. But whether you're going top to bottom double in three or something in between, I want you to choose a three-year target and enter it in to the q12 target section of the planning grid. That's the section on the top right of the tool. When you do, the 12Q planning grid will automatically calculate how much you need to grow each quarter to achieve that three-year target. This calculation assumes a straight line up and to the right growth curve. But as we all know, growth is... is never a straight line. So if you want to get more precise, you can adjust your quarterly target projections using the middle column.
This allows you to factor in launches, seasonality, and even future acquisitions. For example, if sales are slow during the summer months, but come back with a vengeance in the fourth quarter, you can adjust the project. Column to account for those seasonal shifts while still ensuring that you finish above your three-year target line at the end of every quarter. You can enter your real sales and profit figures into the actual column, which will give you a crystal clear picture of whether you are on track, behind, or ahead of your projections. Either way, with this simple tool, you're going to not only document your three-year target, but you're also going to have the insights that you need to do your quarterly sprint planning. Okay. Now that you know how to set your three-year targets, let's move on to the next point of your clarity compass.
